skill.name: caam
skill.description: Account-management documentation for CAAM, a Go CLI that switches AI coding CLI profiles by backing up and restoring local OAuth files.
skill.summary: Manage CAAM profile switching for Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Gemini CLI accounts.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: The scanner findings are partly false positives because many external-command hits are Markdown command examples, not executable skill code. However, the skill intentionally manages OAuth bearer-token files, stores account profiles in a local vault, links sensitive dotfiles into isolated homes, and recommends a remote shell installer, so the confirmed risk remains high.

high_findings.title: Credential-Bearing OAuth File Management
high_findings.description: The skill describes backing up and restoring local OAuth token files for Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Gemini CLI. These files are bearer credentials, and the skill explicitly notes that possession equals access, so vault compromise can expose account access.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly identify auth file paths, describe bearer-token semantics, and define a vault that stores copied credentials. This is confirmed high-risk behavior even though it is the stated purpose of the tool.

high_findings.title: Remote Shell Installer and Privileged Binary Placement
high_findings.description: The installation instructions recommend downloading a remote install script with curl and piping it into bash, then show moving the built binary into /usr/local/bin with sudo. This creates supply-chain and privilege-escalation risk if the remote script, repository, or transport path is compromised.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous pattern is explicit and repeated: a network-fetched script is executed by a shell, and privileged installation is documented. The risk is clear even though the URL points to the project repository.

high_findings.title: Sensitive Dotfile Exposure in Isolated Profiles
high_findings.description: The skill says isolated profiles create a separate HOME and CODEX_HOME while symlinking the real .ssh and .gitconfig files. This may expose SSH keys and Git identity configuration to commands running inside an isolated coding-agent profile.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly names .ssh and .gitconfig symlinks from the real home directory. The exact exposure depends on runtime implementation, but the intended design is plainly documented.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Re-Execution Under Alternate Accounts
medium_findings.description: The run mode describes detecting rate limits, selecting another profile, and re-running the command with a different account. This may surprise users, complicate audit trails, or conflict with provider usage policies when used to continue after a rate limit.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is described directly in workflow documentation. Policy impact depends on provider terms and user account ownership, so this is a medium contextual risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Most External Command Findings Are Documentation Examples
medium_findings.description: Many scanner hits for external commands are Markdown examples such as caam status, caam activate, or shell aliases. They are not executable code inside the skill package, but they can still guide an agent or user to modify local authentication state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line references are visibly command examples in Markdown, so the static command-execution signal is mostly a false positive. The confidence remains high that they are examples, with residual risk from users following them.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Appear to Be False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography findings align with descriptive text and content hashing, including SHA-256 status detection, rather than use of an unsafe cryptographic primitive in executable code. No evidence found of weak cryptography implementation in this skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is documentation and mentions content hashing and health scoring, not executable cryptographic code. Because no source implementation is included, the finding cannot be confirmed from this package.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe Remote Script to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a curl command that retrieves a remote install script and pipes it directly to bash.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both locations contain the same explicit pipe-to-shell installer pattern. This is a well-known dangerous installation pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Credential Vaulting
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs copying local OAuth token files into a CAAM vault for later restoration across profiles.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The vault paths and auth-file names are documented directly. The behavior stores sensitive bearer tokens by design.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Home Directory Symlinks
dangerous_patterns.description: The isolated profile structure links the real .ssh and .gitconfig files into a pseudo-HOME.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly describe and diagram the symlinks. The security impact is credible because those files often contain credentials or sensitive identity configuration.

content.value_statement: Switching between AI coding CLI accounts can interrupt development and create confusing local auth state. This skill documents CAAM workflows for profile vaults, isolated sessions, cooldown tracking, and command usage.
actual_capabilities: Explains CAAM account switching for Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Gemini CLI.
Documents vault profiles that back up and restore local OAuth auth files.
Documents isolated profiles with separate HOME and CODEX_HOME paths.
Lists commands for backup, activation, status checks, cooldowns, and profile rotation.
Describes smart rotation, health scoring, automatic failover, and a terminal dashboard.
limitations: This package contains documentation only, not the CAAM Go source code or binary.
The documented workflows handle sensitive OAuth tokens and require careful local security controls.
Account switching after rate limits may conflict with provider policies or team rules.
The audit cannot verify runtime safeguards because only SKILL.md is present.

use_cases.title: Document local profile switching
use_cases.description: Use the skill to understand CAAM commands for backing up, listing, activating, and clearing local AI CLI profiles.

use_cases.title: Review operational security before rollout
use_cases.description: Use the documented paths and workflows to assess token vault storage, dotfile exposure, installer risk, and policy fit.

use_cases.title: Plan parallel coding-agent sessions
use_cases.description: Use isolated profile guidance to separate Codex or Claude Code sessions across terminals while tracking account context.

prompt_templates.title: Explain CAAM basics
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain what CAAM does, which AI coding CLIs it supports, and what local auth files it manages.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a safe setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a cautious CAAM setup checklist for one Claude Code account and one Codex account, including backup and rollback steps.

prompt_templates.title: Compare profile modes
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare CAAM vault profiles and isolated profiles for my workflow. Include security tradeoffs and when each mode is appropriate.

prompt_templates.title: Audit profile security
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my proposed CAAM operating model for token storage, dotfile symlinks, installer trust, cooldown automation, and provider policy risk.

output_examples.input: Explain the difference between CAAM vault profiles and isolated profiles.
output_examples.output: Vault profiles switch one active account by restoring saved auth files. Isolated profiles run separate sessions with dedicated home paths, but they may still expose linked dotfiles.

output_examples.input: Give me a security checklist before using CAAM.
output: Store the vault on an encrypted local disk.
Avoid syncing OAuth token backups across machines.
Review any remote installer before execution.
Check whether account rotation follows provider and team policies.

output_examples.input: Summarize what files CAAM may touch.
output_examples.output: CAAM may touch Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Gemini CLI auth files, plus its local vault and isolated profile directories.

best_practices: Review the remote installer before running it, or build the CAAM binary from source.
Protect the CAAM vault with local disk encryption and strict file permissions.
Confirm account-switching workflows comply with provider terms and organization policy.
anti_patterns: Do not sync the token vault to shared drives or cloud folders.
Do not run isolated profiles with unnecessary access to SSH keys or sensitive dotfiles.
Do not use automatic failover to hide or ignore account usage limits.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps an assistant explain and apply CAAM documentation for AI coding CLI account profiles.

faq.question: Does the skill include the CAAM binary?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed package contains SKILL.md documentation only.

faq.question: Which tools are documented?
faq.answer: The skill documents Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Gemini CLI profile handling.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: The documented workflows copy OAuth bearer tokens, store them in a vault, and recommend remote shell installation.

faq.question: Can CAAM run multiple accounts at once?
faq.answer: The documentation describes isolated profiles for parallel sessions with separate home and tool directories.

faq.question: What should users verify before use?
faq.answer: Users should verify installer contents, local file permissions, provider policy, and exposure of linked dotfiles.
